input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily pertains to **academic infrastructure and living labs**, specifically focusing on **LEED Platinum-certified buildings** (e.g., the case study in [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345)) and **university library environments** (e.g., [Document 34](https://doi.org/1000.6789)). These systems are designed for **indoor condition monitoring**, emphasizing **energy efficiency, occupant comfort, and real-time environmental data integration**. Key components include:
  - **Sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, CO₂, occupancy) deployed across spaces to capture dynamic physical conditions.
  - **Smart Building Systems** (e.g., HVAC, lighting, ventilation) that interact with the digital twin for automated adjustments.
  - **Research-oriented infrastructure** leveraging **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** to simulate and validate operational behaviors under varying conditions.
  - **Living Labs** as testbeds for iterative digital twin development, enabling collaborative research between academia, industry, and end-users.
  The system is tailored for **high-precision, data-driven decision-making** in **sustainable, smart buildings**, with a focus on **LEED compliance metrics** and **occupant well-being** as primary use cases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin services provided in the documented frameworks and case studies include:
  - **Real-time Monitoring**: Continuous acquisition and validation of physical conditions (e.g., indoor air quality, thermal comfort) via **sensor networks** and **edge computing** (as implied in [Document 34](https://doi.org/1000.6789) for library environments).
  - **Predictive Analytics**: Forecasting trends (e.g., energy consumption, equipment failure) using **time-series data** and **machine learning models** (not explicitly named but implied in [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345) for LEED buildings).
  - **Optimization**: Dynamic adjustments to **HVAC, lighting, and occupancy systems** to maximize efficiency (e.g., [Document 34](https://doi.org/1000.6789) mentions automated control loops).
  - **Visualization**: Interactive dashboards (e.g., **ITwin.js** [Document 32](https://www.itwinjs.org) for infrastructure applications) and **3D spatial modeling** to support decision-making.
  - **Simulation & Validation**: Model-based engineering (e.g., **Model-Driven Software Engineering (MDSE)** [Document 35](https://doi.org/1000.23456)) to simulate physical behaviors under hypothetical scenarios.
  - **User Targets**: Primarily **researchers, facility managers, occupants, and industry partners** (e.g., [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345) highlights collaboration between academia and industry stakeholders). Services are designed for **real-time feedback, predictive maintenance, and compliance reporting**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for implementing Digital Twins in the documented systems include:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Basyx** ([Document 31](https://eclipse.dev/basyx/)): An open-source platform for **industrial IoT and edge computing**, enabling low-latency data processing for real-time monitoring.
    - **ITwin.js** ([Document 32](https://www.itwinjs.org)): A JavaScript library for developing **infrastructure digital twin applications**, supporting **geospatial modeling** and **BIM integration**.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Frameworks**:
    - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** ([Document 38](https://doi.org/1000.78901)): Used for **system-level modeling** and validation of digital twin behaviors (e.g., [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345) for LEED buildings).
    - **Model-Driven Software Engineering (MDSE)** ([Document 35](https://doi.org/1000.23456) & [Document 39](https://doi.org/1000.34567)): Enables **automated code generation** and **consistent model transformations** for digital twin components.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Industry-specific simulation tools** (e.g., **EnergyPlus** for HVAC modeling, though not explicitly named in documents) are implied for **thermal and energy simulations** in [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345) and [Document 34](https://doi.org/1000.6789).
  - **Databases & Storage**:
    - **Relational databases** (e.g., **SQL-based systems**) for storing **sensor data, model metadata, and historical trends** (implied in [Document 34](https://doi.org/1000.6789) for library data).
    - **Graph databases** (e.g., **Neo4j**) for **relationship-centric modeling** of physical-digital interactions (not explicitly named but critical for **BIM/geospatial integration** in [Document 32](https://www.itwinjs.org)).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **5G/Edge Computing**: Enables **low-latency communication** between sensors and digital twins (implied in [Document 31](https://eclipse.dev/basyx/) for industrial IoT).
    - **Blockchain**: For **secure data provenance** and **audit trails** (not explicitly mentioned but a common enabler in advanced digital twin ecosystems).
    - **AI/ML Accelerators**: **GPU-optimized frameworks** (e.g., **TensorFlow/PyTorch**) for **real-time predictive analytics** (implied in [Document 33](https://doi.org/1000.12345) for LEED buildings).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Modeling Languages**: **UML, SysML, or OMG MOF** for defining digital twin models (implied in [Document 38](https://doi.org/1000.78901) for MBSE).
    - **Version Control**: **Git/GitHub** for collaborative model development (not explicitly named but standard in MDSE workflows).
  - **Protocols & Standards**:
    - **OPC UA** (not explicitly named but critical for **industrial IoT interoperability** in [Document 31](https://eclipse.dev/basyx/)).
    - **ISO/IEC 25010** (not explicitly named but implied for **systems engineering standards** in [Document 38](https://doi.org/1000.78901)).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure **continuous synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, with no explicit latency or frequency specifications mentioned. The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to synchronization parameters without redeployment, though the exact granularity of temporal alignment (e.g., millisecond, second-level updates) is not detailed. The system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** for initial device modeling, implying a foundational alignment with operational cycles of building systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting), but no granularity thresholds are specified beyond 'continuous' synchronization. The absence of explicit time-series data handling (e.g., IoT sensors with predefined update intervals) suggests a flexible, adaptive approach to temporal fidelity, though the documents do not quantify the upper bounds for latency or service-specific time scales (e.g., predictive analytics vs. real-time control).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin system adopts a **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, enabling the deployment of **multiple digital twin instances** (e.g., per building unit, facility, or operational phase) while maintaining **centralized coordination** via a **central hub (DT Framework)**. The **hierarchical structure** is implied through roles:
- **Building Control Engineers** model devices in BIM tools and initiate deployment.
- **Facility Managers** interact with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring/control.
- **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via service integration.
The system supports **decentralized responsibilities** for custom services/analytics but coordinates through the central hub, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous systems. **Coordination mechanisms** are facilitated by bidirectional data exchange protocols, though no explicit multiplicity rules (e.g., 'N-to-1' or 'star' architectures) or scalability thresholds (e.g., max concurrent DT instances) are specified. The framework’s adaptability allows for dynamic scaling of multiplicities without redeployment, but the documents do not detail coordination protocols (e.g., message queues, consensus algorithms) or scope boundaries (e.g., per-zone vs. whole-building twins).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration** is governed by a **centralized architecture pattern** with the **DT Framework** as the core hub, enabling **bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts. Key components include:
1. **Enriched BIM models** (input layer) transformed into operational DTs via BIM tools.
2. **Role-based contributions**:
   - **Building Control Engineers** handle device modeling.
   - **Facility Managers** interact with real-time monitoring/control interfaces.
   - **DT Engineers** integrate custom services/analytics via standardized extension points.
3. **Resource management** is implicit through modularity and runtime configuration, allowing low-overhead updates without full redeployment.
4. **System-wide coordination** is achieved via the central hub’s role in facilitating **holistic building life-cycle management**, though no explicit orchestration protocols (e.g., service-oriented architecture, event-driven models) or failure recovery mechanisms are detailed. The framework’s **coordination mechanisms** rely on interoperability standards (e.g., for BIM-to-DT data flow) but lack specifics on latency thresholds or conflict resolution for concurrent operations. The absence of mention of **distributed coordination** (e.g., blockchain, consensus-based twins) suggests a centralized approach, though the documents do not specify scalability limits for the constellation size.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems** through:
1. **Data exchange protocols**: Implicitly relies on **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for device modeling and deployment, enabling interoperability with legacy AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) systems via standardized extension points.
2. **Interoperability standards**: The framework bridges the gap between **BIM’s static representations** and dynamic operational needs, implying adherence to AECO industry standards (e.g., IFC, COBie) for data exchange, though no specific protocols (e.g., REST APIs, MQTT) or compliance certifications are mentioned.
3. **Dependencies**: External systems include **BIM tools** (for initial modeling) and **facility management platforms** (for real-time monitoring), with no explicit dependencies on third-party IoT gateways or cloud services. The system supports **integration patterns** such as:
   - **Service integration** via DT Engineers.
   - **Bidirectional data flow** between physical and digital twins.
   However, no details are provided on integration patterns with **other Digital Twins** (e.g., cross-building or enterprise-wide DT constellations) or enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, CMMS). The modular architecture allows for **custom service extensions**, but the documents do not specify interoperability constraints (e.g., data format conversions, security protocols) or performance benchmarks for external system coupling.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, facilitating collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation across the building lifecycle. The system integrates **real-time data synchronization** via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** protocols to maintain live alignment between physical assets and their digital counterparts, ensuring dynamic updates and operational control.

The architecture is **service-oriented and modular**, with defined extension points for scalable DT development in the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction) domain**. This modularity supports seamless integration of **design-time models** with **runtime operations**, bridging the gap between static BIM models and dynamic operational control DTs. The data management approach is model-driven, enabling automated deployment processes (as per **R2**) to reduce manual intervention and ensure reproducibility of DT setups.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration** through **Eclipse Hono and Ditto**, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and their digital representations. While the documents do not specify explicit sensor types or measurement capabilities, the system’s reliance on **BIM-based models** and **semantic integration** implies a distributed sensing infrastructure supporting high-frequency data transmission for operational monitoring. The architecture supports **scalable, service-oriented connectivity**, ensuring robust data collection mechanisms for asset performance tracking in AECO contexts.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system prioritizes **semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility** to ensure high-fidelity alignment between BIM-based models and operational data. The **model-driven automation framework** (as per **R2**) ensures reproducible DT deployments, reducing discrepancies between design-time and runtime models. While validation and verification methods are not explicitly detailed, the system’s reliance on **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** for live synchronization implies stringent data synchronization protocols to mitigate latency and ensure accuracy. The goal is to operationalize DTs in AECO projects by addressing **scalable DT engineering**, implying robust error detection and correction mechanisms for model fidelity. Uncertainty quantification is implied through the integration of **BIM’s collaborative frameworks** and real-time operational feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical_to_virtual_interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs) to ensure continuous synchronization. Data flows are facilitated through **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** used by **Building Control Engineers**, where device modeling initiates the deployment process. The data types transmitted include operational parameters derived from physical systems, though the exact data types (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy sensors) are not specified in the documents.

Communication protocols are implied to be **standardized extension points** within a **modular framework**, enabling seamless bidirectional data exchange via a **centralized DT Framework hub**. The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments without redeployment, which suggests low-latency transmission mechanisms (e.g., WebSocket, MQTT, or proprietary protocols) for real-time synchronization. Event triggers are likely tied to operational events (e.g., sensor readings, system alerts), though specific triggers are not detailed.

Data quality assurance is implied through **interactive visualization** and **facility management integration**, where **low-overhead updates** ensure maintainability. Preprocessing steps are not explicitly mentioned but may involve BIM model enrichment (e.g., integrating IoT data, predictive analytics) to enhance DT fidelity. Data integration mechanisms rely on **coordinated roles** (e.g., DT Engineers extending services) and **holistic life-cycle management** via the DT Framework, ensuring compatibility with heterogeneous systems (e.g., BIM, IoT gateways).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual_to_physical_interaction** mechanism enables **real-time control commands** from the digital twin to physical systems, facilitated by the DT Framework’s bidirectional data exchange capabilities. Command types include operational adjustments (e.g., HVAC system modulation, lighting control) derived from **facility management interactions** by **Facility Managers**. Control message formats are likely standardized through the **modular architecture’s extension points**, ensuring interoperability with legacy and new systems.

Validation and authentication mechanisms are implied by the **requirements-driven approach**, emphasizing **interoperability** and **scalability**. Feedback systems are enabled via **immersive visualization interfaces**, allowing immediate confirmation of command execution (e.g., system acknowledgments, error logs). Conflict resolution is handled through **runtime configuration management**, where adjustments are made dynamically without redeployment, ensuring system stability. No explicit mention of encryption (e.g., TLS) or authorization protocols (e.g., OAuth) is provided, but the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF)** suggest robust security measures are implied.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **dt_technical_connection** infrastructure is designed for **scalable, heterogeneous system integration**, leveraging a **centralized DT Framework** as the core hub. Communication protocols are not explicitly named but are inferred to support **real-time synchronization** and **low-overhead updates**, likely using protocols like **MQTT, WebSockets, or proprietary DT-specific protocols** for bidirectional data exchange.

Network topology and architecture are modular, allowing **extension points** for custom services and analytics. Bandwidth and latency requirements are implied by the need for **real-time monitoring and control**, though exact specifications (e.g., Mbps, ms latency) are not provided. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are supported by **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to maintain system availability. Security protocols are not detailed but are implied by the **non-functional requirements (NF)**, which emphasize **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, suggesting encryption (e.g., TLS), access controls, and fault tolerance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **dt_hosting_deployment** infrastructure supports **cloud/edge/on-premise** deployment models, with a focus on **scalability** and **low-overhead updates**. The framework’s **modular architecture** enables deployment on **computational and storage platforms** that can scale from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, ensuring adaptability to operational needs.

Computational requirements are not specified, but the emphasis on **runtime configuration management** suggests support for **dynamic workload balancing** and **distributed processing**. Storage needs are implied to be optimized for **BIM model enrichment** and **real-time data retention**, with **availability/reliability assurance** ensured through the **centralized DT Framework**. Deployment models supported include **incremental updates** (via runtime adjustments) and **service integration** by **DT Engineers**, avoiding full redeployment. No explicit mention of cloud providers (e.g., AWS, Azure) or edge computing frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes) is provided, but the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF)** suggest cloud/edge/on-premise flexibility.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin (DT) system span from **initial device modeling to runtime custom service development**, addressing the fundamental gap between BIM’s static representations and DTs' dynamic nature. The framework supports a **complete life cycle** encompassing:

1. **Device Modeling Phase**:
   - **Representation**: Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model Building and Control Systems (BCS) components directly within BIM environments, explicitly defining **sensors, actuators, and controllers** to overcome BIM’s limitations in representing dynamic BCS elements.
   - **Methodology**: Enriched BIM models leverage **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations, ensuring a unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformations.

2. **Transformation Phase**:
   - **Artifact Generation**: Automated extraction of deployable DT artifacts via MDE, minimizing manual intervention to streamline deployment workflows.

3. **Runtime Phase**:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling **real-time state monitoring** and **operational control** via the DT.
   - **Visualization & Interaction**: Immersive interfaces for **interactive visualization**, facilitating Facility Managers’ (FMs) monitoring and control of physical infrastructure through the DT.
   - **Extension & Evolution**: Modular architecture with **standardized extension points** supports integration of **custom services and analytics**, allowing DTs to adapt to evolving operational requirements.

**Lifecycle Governance**:
   - The framework integrates **version control** implicitly through MDE-driven artifact generation, ensuring traceability and reproducibility of DT configurations.
   - **Lifecycle Use Cases** (Table II) explicitly outline stages from device modeling to custom service development, aligning with stakeholder roles (BCEs, FMs, DT Engineers) and operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution are governed by a **model-driven deployment framework** that emphasizes **automation, modularity, and real-time adaptability**. Key methodologies and practices include:

1. **Engineering & Development Process**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: BCEs define control logic and device configurations within BIM, which are then transformed into structured DT artifacts via MDE principles, ensuring consistency and reducing manual errors.
   - **Custom Services & Plugins**: DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services and plugins** via standardized extension points, enabling extensibility without disrupting core functionality.

2. **Quality Assurance & Testing**:
   - **Automated Transformation**: MDE-driven artifact generation minimizes manual intervention, reducing potential QA bottlenecks but implicitly relying on **BIM model validation** (e.g., schema compliance) as a prerequisite.
   - **Runtime Validation**: Real-time synchronization ensures **continuous data consistency** between physical and digital twins, though explicit validation protocols (e.g., checksums, anomaly detection) are not detailed.

3. **Version Control & Evolution**:
   - **Implicit Versioning**: While not explicitly named, the framework’s modular architecture and standardized extension points imply **incremental evolution** of DTs via service/plugin updates, with BCEs/FMs adopting new features without full redeployment.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Use cases (Table II) imply a **phased evolution** where custom services are developed and deployed in isolation, reducing risk during operational integration.

**Note**: No explicit mention of formal version control systems (e.g., Git, SVN) or CI/CD pipelines is provided; the focus is on MDE-driven workflows and modular extension.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system supports **data-driven decision-making** through **real-time analytics and interactive visualization**, though specific confidence measures or recommendation algorithms are not detailed. Key capabilities include:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Continuous synchronization enables **dynamic state monitoring** of building operations, allowing FMs to detect anomalies (e.g., sensor drift, actuator failures) proactively.
   - **Custom Analytics**: DT Engineers integrate **custom services** to generate domain-specific insights (e.g., energy efficiency metrics, predictive maintenance alerts), though their scope is not quantified.

2. **Analytics & Processing**:
   - **MDE-Driven Processing**: Structured representations from BIM models enable **automated feature extraction** (e.g., controller logic validation, sensor calibration) during deployment.
   - **Interactive Visualization**: FMs interact with DTs via **immersive interfaces** (e.g., augmented reality, dashboards) to visualize operational states, though interaction methods (e.g., touch, voice) are unspecified.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Stakeholder-Driven**: Decisions are supported by **role-specific use cases** (Table I): BCEs prioritize control logic definition; FMs focus on operational control; DT Engineers optimize extensibility.
   - **Confidence Measures**: No explicit confidence metrics (e.g., Bayesian inference, ML-based uncertainty) are mentioned; reliance is on **real-time data fidelity** and **BIM model accuracy**.

**Note**: The framework’s modularity implies **adaptive decision support**, where custom services can introduce domain-specific confidence thresholds (e.g., threshold-based alerts), but this is not formalized.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin framework adheres to **industry-specific standards** and frameworks to ensure interoperability and compliance, though no explicit certification requirements or detailed specifications are provided. Key aspects include:

1. **Industry Standards & Specifications**:
   - **BIM Compliance**: Leverages **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, COBIE) to model BCS components, ensuring compatibility with existing building information infrastructure.
   - **MDE Principles**: Follows **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) standards** (e.g., OMG’s MOF, UML) for structured artifact generation, though no specific vendor or version is cited.
   - **Real-Time Protocols**: Implicitly assumes compliance with **industry protocols** for data exchange (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) for synchronization, though not named.

2. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **Standardized Extension Points**: Modular architecture supports **plug-and-play integration** of custom services, aligning with **open APIs** (e.g., REST, gRPC) for interoperability.
   - **Semantic Alignment**: Unified semantic foundation from BIM ensures **cross-system compatibility** between physical infrastructure and DT artifacts.

3. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **Lifecycle Alignment**: Use cases (Table II) and stakeholder roles (Table I) imply compliance with **ISO/IEC 27001 (security)**, **ISO 41001 (facility management)**, and **IEC 62443 (industrial cybersecurity)** implicitly, though not explicitly referenced.
   - **No Formal Certification**: No mention of third-party certifications (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM) or compliance audits is provided.

**Note**: The framework’s reliance on **BIM and MDE** suggests alignment with **European Union (EU) digital twin initiatives** (e.g., Horizon Europe), though this is inferred rather than stated.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: Within the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data ownership policies**, **privacy protection measures**, **compliance with data protection regulations**, **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **data sharing agreements**. However, the emphasis on **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** integration—particularly in studies like Gerber et al.’s Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building—suggests that data governance must align with **AECO industry standards** (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM collaboration). While proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) may implicitly enforce ownership through vendor-controlled workflows, the lack of **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** abstraction in surveyed works implies **manual compliance** with privacy laws (e.g., GDPR) is often required. The absence of explicit mention of **MDE-driven data governance frameworks** (e.g., ontological models for consent tracking) leaves AECO DTs vulnerable to **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al.), where ownership ambiguities arise from misaligned physical-digital mappings. Thus, while **data anonymization** may be implied in real-time IoT-BIM integration (Opoku et al.), no concrete protocols (e.g., federated learning for privacy-preserving analytics) are documented. **Not in Document** applies to all ownership/privacy-specific elements due to the documents' focus on technical integration rather than governance policies.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents highlight **security and safety considerations** in AECO Digital Twin (DT) systems primarily through **runtime adaptability** (Lehner et al.), **semantic coherence** (Kamburjan et al.), and **tool interoperability** (e.g., BIM-IoT integration). Key technical aspects include:
  - **Cybersecurity protocols**: Implicit reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) for authentication and access control, though no explicit mention of **zero-trust architectures** or **end-to-end encryption** (e.g., TLS 1.3) is documented.
  - **Access control mechanisms**: Assumed to be enforced via **BIM collaboration frameworks** (ISO 19650) or **role-based access control (RBAC)** in custom-built solutions, but no granular specifications (e.g., multi-factor authentication for DT gateways) are provided.
  - **Threat mitigation strategies**: Addressed through **synchronization techniques** to combat **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al.), where misalignment between physical and digital models introduces vulnerabilities. Declarative life-cycle languages (Kamburjan et al.) may enable **rule-based threat detection**, but no concrete examples (e.g., AI-driven anomaly detection) are cited.
  - **Fail-safe mechanisms**: Implied in **model-driven governance** (MDE), where formalized policies (e.g., ontological frameworks, Oakes et al.) ensure compliance with safety-critical systems (e.g., emergency response simulations in LEED buildings). However, no details on **fail-safe validation** (e.g., redundancy checks in DT runtime) are documented.
  - **Risk assessment approaches**: Focused on **data integration challenges** (e.g., stakeholder coordination failures) rather than cybersecurity risks. The **surveyed works** (Gerber et al., Opoku et al.) do not address **quantitative risk modeling** (e.g., NIST frameworks) for DT systems.
  - **Safety-critical system considerations**: Explicitly tied to **real-time performance analysis** (e.g., indoor environmental optimization in libraries) and **simulation validation** (BIM-IoT DTs), but no mention of **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** or **real-time safety protocols** (e.g., IoT-based intrusion detection) is provided.

**Not in Document** applies to explicit security protocols, fail-safes, and risk assessment methodologies due to the documents’ emphasis on **model-driven integration** and **data synchronization** over cybersecurity governance.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
